project_overview.project_development_objective: To increase the number of farmers and rural households benefiting from commercial agriculture value-chains in Program's areas
project_overview.total_financing: US$100 million IDA (US$50 million Credit + US$50 million Grant) for Phase 1; Overall MPA envelope US$200 million IDA with US$82 million from other sources
project_overview.beneficiaries: 24,000 jobs created (12,000 for women); 145,000 farmers and rural households (72,000 women) provided with commercial opportunities; approximately 347,000 beneficiaries of rehabilitated roads; 25,000 direct beneficiaries from infrastructure investments

geographic_scope.coverage_type: regional with potential national expansion in Phase 2
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: partial - Guinea is classified as fragile with high political and governance risks; focus on mining regions (Boké and Kindia) where social tensions exist due to mining activities and lack of inclusive development opportunities

key_components.component_name: Component 1: Improving Market Access in Targeted Areas
key_components.description: Rehabilitation of 800 km of rural roads in Phase 1 (600 km in Boké and Kindia, 200 km for specific private investments); establishment of agri-logistics centers, small-scale slaughterhouses, and complementary facilities; support for sustainable management of market infrastructure through cost-recovery mechanisms and private operators
key_components.financing: US$57 million (Phase 1); US$174 million overall MPA

key_components.description: Dedicated Financing Facility (DFF) with three windows: Window A (large investments >US$1M, 13 subprojects), Window B (medium US$100K-1M, 200 subprojects), Window C (small up to US$100K, 1,000 subprojects); business development services; technical assistance for investors; focus on women and youth entrepreneurs
key_components.financing: US$30 million (Phase 1); US$69 million overall MPA

key_components.component_name: Component 3: Enabling Environment for Commercial Agriculture
key_components.description: Strengthening public agencies (APIP, AGUIPEX, SNCPA); strengthening coordination along value chains through interprofessional associations; enhancing SPS control, quality standards, and food safety systems; upgrading laboratories; developing market information systems
key_components.financing: US$12 million (Phase 1); US$22 million overall MPA

key_components.description: Mechanism for funding emergency response and recovery from natural disasters and other shocks; no funds allocated upfront but available for reallocation if crisis develops

Establishment and operation of Project Coordination and Implementation Unit (PCIU) within Ministry of Agriculture; two regional operational units in Boké and Kindia; financial management, procurement, M&E, safeguards compliance, grievance redress mechanism, communication and outreach
key_components.financing: US$8 million (Phase 1); US$17 million overall MPA

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Agriculture (MINAGRI) through a dedicated Project Coordination and Implementation Unit (PCIU); implementing partners include APIP, AGUIPEX, ANAFIC, DNGR, local governments, and private operators
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: Project Inter-ministerial Steering and Coordination Committee (PISCO) chaired by high-level government official; Project Technical Coordination Committee (PTECO) chaired by Secretary General of MINAGRI; two regional operational units in Boké and Kindia; coordination with IFC and other development partners
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations for IPF Borrowers (July 2016, revised 2017-2018); competitive procurement for goods, works, and services; prior review for contracts above thresholds; use of STEP (Systematic Tracking of Exchanges in Procurement); international competitive bidding for complex works (slaughterhouses, laboratories); specialized agribusiness firm to support implementation
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Robust M&E system with baseline and follow-up surveys; disaggregation by gender and age; semi-annual progress reports; impact evaluation during Phase 1 to inform Phase 2 design; mid-term review by end of year 3; final evaluation in last semester of implementation; M&E specialist within PCIU; integration with MINAGRI's overall M&E system

identified_risks.risk_description: Turbulent political history with elections planned for 2020; potential for difficult transition that could slow or complicate implementation; governance and rule of law issues affecting contract enforcement and trade policies
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ensure efficient project start-up and establish solid implementation base prior to presidential campaign; involve large private companies and local governments for social control; support from high-level PISCO; policy dialogue through DPOs

identified_risks.risk_description: Economy vulnerable to external shocks, particularly commodity price swings; COVID-19 pandemic reducing growth to 1.4% in 2020; potential for lower aluminum prices affecting current account; social distancing reducing labor supply
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: WBG and IMF monitoring of macroeconomic developments; IMF Rapid Credit Facility (US$148 million) and debt relief; WBG COVID-19 Crisis Response financing; financing partnership WBG/IFC/IMF

identified_risks.risk_description: Low institutional capacity for implementation; weak skill level of private sector operators; difficulties in disbursement and implementation from past projects; slow project start-up and lack of preparedness
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Independent PCIU within MINAGRI guided by high-level PISCO and PTECO; specialized international agribusiness firm to support implementation; capacity building and training for relevant institutions; regular independent technical reviews/audits; frequent World Bank implementation support missions; detailed action plans prepared during preparation

identified_risks.risk_description: Weak FM arrangements at MINAGRI; inadequate staffing and FM tools; lack of accounting software and procedures; weak internal audit function; multiplicity of actors involved in transactions; two regional operational units; inherent country-level governance risks
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Ring-fenced financing mechanism for Project; recruitment of FM Officer, Chief Accountant, and two Accountants before/after effectiveness; acquisition and installation of accounting software; operationalization of internal audit function; recruitment of external auditor; detailed FM procedures manual; DA at Central Bank; quarterly IFRs and annual audited financial statements; FM Action Plan with specific deadlines

identified_risks.risk_description: New PCIU will need to coordinate procurement with multiple implementing partners; MINAGRI has limited procurement capacity and experience with WB procedures; DNCMP involvement adds complexity; significant delays common in past projects
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Recruitment of Procurement Specialist before effectiveness; elaboration of PIM with procurement procedures; training for tender committee and partners; World Bank team support; use of STEP; technical assistance from ACGP for feasibility studies and supervision; prior review of contracts above thresholds

identified_risks.risk_description: Category A project with potential for significant environmental and social impacts; involuntary resettlement from infrastructure development; land acquisition; impacts on natural habitats and forests; pest management and pesticide use; physical cultural resources; international waterways; gender-based violence and sexual exploitation risks; COVID-19 outbreak risks
identified_risks.risk_rating: substantial (rated as high for E&S in SORT)
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: ESMF, Resettlement Policy Framework, and PPMP prepared and disclosed; site-specific ESIAs and RAPs to be prepared once locations determined; recruitment of Environmental and Social Safeguards Specialists before effectiveness; capacity building and training for stakeholders; GRM established; SEA/SH assessment and action plan; COVID-19 procedures; chance find procedures for cultural resources; forest restoration activities; screening for critical natural habitats; compliance with CFS-RAI principles

identified_risks.risk_description: Proposed reforms may encounter resistance from vested interests and established practices within administration; critical reforms enabling environment for commercial agriculture need policy dialogue support
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Support through WBG policy dialogue with GoG via DPOs; Component 3 activities to strengthen enabling environment; public-private dialogue facilitated through IFC ICA Project; capacity building for key institutions

identified_risks.risk_description: COVID-19 pandemic creating uncertainties; impacts on labor force availability; potential to disrupt supply chains globally; could negatively impact project completion timelines
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Close follow-up of National Emergency Preparedness and Response Plan implementation by Government and World Bank; specific COVID-19 procedures for project sites; designated COVID-19 focal points; worker induction and toolbox meetings on COVID-19 prevention; regular audits and inspections; support for affected individuals

safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Project-level GRM established within PCIU and building on existing GRM at APIP and ANAFIC; covers difficulties in obtaining project support and adverse impacts from project activities; confidential handling of sensitive complaints (SEA/SH, child exploitation); referral to relevant authorities where necessary; World Bank's corporate GRS and Inspection Panel also available
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Gender analysis conducted during preparation; gender-sensitive training and balanced participation; specific attention to GBV and child exploitation; Window C of DFF prioritizes women with higher co-funding (90% vs 60%); women trained on business capacities (target 600); new agribusinesses led by women (target 180); female-headed households' labor productivity 8% less than male-headed; women's lower education (24.7% literacy vs 54.9% for men); only 9% of Guinean firms have women owners; gender gaps in bank account ownership; all bidding documents include GBV commitment statements; contractors required to adopt codes of conduct; regular audits for SEA/SH and child exploitation
vulnerable_groups: Women (particularly female-headed households and women entrepreneurs)
Communities in mining areas (Boké and Kindia) experiencing social tensions
Communities affected by natural habitats conversion
Children (protection from child labor and exploitation)

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly addresses fragility and conflict drivers: high youth unemployment (24,000 jobs created), economic diversification beyond mining (reducing vulnerability to external shocks), inclusive development in mining areas (alleviating social tensions and frustration), support for women and youth economic opportunities. Project designed to build trust with private investors through longer-term MPA commitment despite fragile governance institutions. Involvement of mining companies in community development initiatives. Focus on domestic value chains initially to build resilience before export orientation.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: Guinea classified as fragile with high political and governance risks; turbulent political history with internal crises and conflicts in neighboring countries (Liberia, Sierra Leone, Côte d'Ivoire); 2013 Ebola outbreak (2014-2015) devastated economy; high poverty (57.7% in 2014, rising from 55% in 2012); youth underemployment major driver of fragility; overdependency on mining sector creates vulnerability to external shocks; weak governance (Rule of Law, Control of Corruption, Government Effectiveness below 15th percentile); poor business environment (156 among 190 in Doing Business); mining sector growth without inclusive development creating social unrest; 2020 COVID-19 pandemic further straining fragile economy
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: Project located in mining regions (Boké and Kindia) where mining activities attract large inflows of people seeking employment, creating social unrest; project aims to alleviate this through agricultural and rural activities. Infrastructure development (roads, markets) may improve security through better connectivity and reduced isolation of rural communities. Food safety and SPS systems strengthen resilience to zoonotic disease outbreaks (Ebola, COVID-19 prevention). Labor-intensive public works provide income opportunities reducing vulnerability to recruitment into illicit activities.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - Involuntary resettlement expected from infrastructure development (rural roads rehabilitation, market infrastructure construction, slaughterhouses, logistics centers); OP/BP 4.12 triggered; Resettlement Policy Framework prepared and disclosed; location-specific RAPs to be developed once sites determined; physical and economic displacement anticipated; land acquisition required; compensation and livelihood restoration support planned; census and asset enumeration to be conducted; meaningful consultation with local communities required; specific social assessment for mining firms to understand community needs
